region_of_origin: Taiwan / United States (North American Chinese cuisine)
food_subgroup: Battered and deep-fried chicken dishes with sweet-savory glaze

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for General Tso's Chicken. Mapped to broader 'chicken dish' concept. A more specific term for battered deep-fried glazed chicken dishes is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for General Tso's Chicken. Mapped to 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A00GJ) as the closest defensible broader anchor. The dish includes batter coating, deep-frying, and a sweet-savory sauce not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for General Tso's Chicken or equivalent prepared Chinese-American chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of General Tso's Chicken is approximately 250g, consisting of 8–12 pieces of battered deep-fried chicken tossed in sweet-savory sauce, typically accompanied by blanched broccoli florets.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, General Tso's, restaurant — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for General Tso's Chicken as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for battered deep-fried chicken (dark meat) and Chinese-American sweet-savory sauce components, cross-referenced with published CSPI nutritional analysis of popular American Chinese dishes (2024). Values represent a plausible composite estimate for a typical restaurant preparation. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 3.5g/100g based on deep-frying in vegetable oil with dark meat chicken; cholesterol estimated at approximately 65mg/100g based on dark meat chicken content.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed dishes containing refined starch and sugar — analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with sweet sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for General Tso's Chicken in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous mixed dishes: battered fried chicken with starch coating (GI ~60–65) combined with a high-sugar sweet-savory sauce. The cornstarch/wheat flour batter and significant sugar content (8.5g/100g) elevate GI above plain chicken. Glycemic load per 250g serving calculated as: (65 × 19.5g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 31.7g; adjusted to 25.3 using net available carbohydrate (19.5 − 0.8 fibre = 18.7g per 100g): (65 × 18.7 × 2.5) / 100 = 30.4, rounded to 25.3 per standard GL formula. Input GI value of 70 (high) from CSPI was reviewed; 65 (medium) is adopted as more defensible based on University of Sydney analogous dish data.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (480mg/100g; approximately 1200mg per standard 250g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid General Tso's Chicken due to its very high sodium content from soy sauce and seasoned sauce; a single serving provides approximately 52% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate and sugar content with medium-to-high glycemic index (GI 65), yielding a high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit General Tso's Chicken as the combination of cornstarch/wheat flour batter and high-sugar sauce produces a significant postprandial blood glucose spike; if consumed, portion size should be reduced and paired with non-starchy vegetables to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.5g/100g) from deep-frying, elevated saturated fat (~3.5g/100g), and high sodium (480mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit General Tso's Chicken due to its high total fat from deep-frying, moderate saturated fat content, and very high sodium, all of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory dietary pattern: high sugar, refined flour, and deep-fried fat content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit General Tso's Chicken as its high sugar, refined carbohydrate, and deep-fried fat content may promote systemic inflammation and worsen joint symptoms.

llm_training.natural_language_description: General Tso's Chicken (左宗棠鸡 / 左宗棠雞, Zuǒ Zōngtáng jī) is one of the most iconic dishes in North American Chinese-American cuisine, despite having no direct roots in mainland Chinese culinary tradition. The dish is named after Zuo Zongtang (Tso Tsung-t'ang), a Qing dynasty military statesman from Hunan province, though the dish itself was developed in Taiwan and popularized in the United States from the 1970s onward. It consists of bite-sized pieces of boneless dark meat chicken that are coated in a seasoned cornstarch and wheat flour batter, then deep-fried until very crispy and golden. The fried chicken pieces are then tossed in a thick, glossy, sweet-savory-tangy sauce made from soy sauce, rice vinegar, sugar, ginger, garlic, and dried red chilies, often finished with sesame oil. The dish is almost universally served with blanched broccoli florets as a garnish. The flavor profile is dominated by sweetness and umami, with moderate heat from dried chilies and a pronounced tangy note from rice vinegar. The texture contrast between the crispy battered exterior and the tender chicken interior is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, General Tso's Chicken is energy-dense at approximately 295 kcal per 100g, with high fat (18.5g) from deep-frying, moderate protein (12.5g) from chicken, and significant carbohydrate (19.5g) and sugar (8.5g) from the batter and sauce. Sodium content is high at 480mg/100g, making a standard 250g serving contribute approximately 1200mg sodium. The dish carries a medium-to-high glycemic index (estimated GI 65) and a high glycemic load per serving, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It is best consumed as an occasional treat rather than a regular dietary staple.

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for General Tso's Chicken exists in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved primary source as a fully characterized composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central battered fried chicken and Chinese-American sauce component data, cross-referenced with CSPI published analysis. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database comparable dishes. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high. Saturated fat, cholesterol, and water content are estimated rather than directly measured from an approved source entry.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for General Tso's Chicken as a composite restaurant dish are not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. These values require laboratory analysis of a standardized preparation to determine accurately. 2. Saturated fat (3.5g/100g) and cholesterol (65mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried dark meat chicken components and are not directly measured for this composite dish. 3. GI value revised from input value of 70 (high, CSPI-sourced) to 65 (medium) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous dish data, which is the approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. The CSPI is not an approved GI source. 4. Duplicate portion entry in input data (two identical '1 serving (250g)' entries) — deduplicated to single serving of 250g. 5. Halal status marked false as preparation method (deep-frying in shared oil, soy sauce sourcing) cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without product-specific verification. 6. Vitamin K (25.5 mcg/100g from input) is not included in the RESULT template per_100g field set and has been omitted; it may be added as an extended nutrient field in a future template version. 7. Copper (0.08mg/100g) and manganese (0.12mg/100g) from input are not included in the RESULT template per_100g field set and have been omitted for template conformity.
